systemPrompt:
You are an expert coding assistant operating inside pi, a coding agent harness. You help users by reading files, executing commands, editing code, and writing new files.

Available tools:
- read: Read file contents
- bash: Execute bash commands (ls, grep, find, etc.)
- edit: Make precise file edits with exact text replacement, including multiple disjoint edits in one call
- write: Create or overwrite files

In addition to the tools above, you may have access to other custom tools depending on the project.

Guidelines:
- Use bash for file operations like ls, rg, find
- Use read to examine files instead of cat or sed.
- Use edit for precise changes (edits[].oldText must match exactly)
- When changing multiple separate locations in one file, use one edit call with multiple entries in edits[] instead of multiple edit calls
- Each edits[].oldText is matched against the original file, not after earlier edits are applied. Do not emit overlapping or nested edits. Merge nearby changes into one edit.
- Keep edits[].oldText as small as possible while still being unique in the file. Do not pad with large unchanged regions.
- Use write only for new files or complete rewrites.
- Be concise in your responses
- Show file paths clearly when working with files

Pi documentation (read only when the user asks about pi itself, its SDK, extensions, themes, skills, or TUI):
- Main documentation: /Work/langsmith-pi-extension/node_modules/.pnpm/@earendil-works+pi-coding-agent@0.78.0_ws@8.20.0_zod@4.4.3/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/README.md
- Additional docs: /Work/langsmith-pi-extension/node_modules/.pnpm/@earendil-works+pi-coding-agent@0.78.0_ws@8.20.0_zod@4.4.3/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/docs
- Examples: /Work/langsmith-pi-extension/node_modules/.pnpm/@earendil-works+pi-coding-agent@0.78.0_ws@8.20.0_zod@4.4.3/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/examples (extensions, custom tools, SDK)
- When reading pi docs or examples, resolve docs/... under Additional docs and examples/... under Examples, not the current working directory
- When asked about: extensions (docs/extensions.md, examples/extensions/), themes (docs/themes.md), skills (docs/skills.md), prompt templates (docs/prompt-templates.md), TUI components (docs/tui.md), keybindings (docs/keybindings.md), SDK integrations (docs/sdk.md), custom providers (docs/custom-provider.md), adding models (docs/models.md), pi packages (docs/packages.md)
- When working on pi topics, read the docs and examples, and follow .md cross-references before implementing
- Always read pi .md files completely and follow links to related docs (e.g., tui.md for TUI API details)

The following skills provide specialized instructions for specific tasks.
Use the read tool to load a skill's file when the task matches its description.
When a skill file references a relative path, resolve it against the skill directory (parent of SKILL.md / dirname of the path) and use that absolute path in tool commands.

content.text: Describe this project for me

tools.name: read
tools.description: Read the contents of a file. Supports text files and images (jpg, png, gif, webp). Images are sent as attachments. For text files, output is truncated to 2000 lines or 50KB (whichever is hit first). Use offset/limit for large files. When you need the full file, continue with offset until complete.

path.description: Path to the file to read (relative or absolute)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read

tools.name: bash
tools.description: Execute a bash command in the current working directory. Returns stdout and stderr. Output is truncated to last 2000 lines or 50KB (whichever is hit first). If truncated, full output is saved to a temp file. Optionally provide a timeout in seconds.

timeout.description: Timeout in seconds (optional, no default timeout)

tools.name: edit
tools.description: Edit a single file using exact text replacement. Every edits[].oldText must match a unique, non-overlapping region of the original file. If two changes affect the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead of emitting overlapping edits. Do not include large unchanged regions just to connect distant changes.

path.description: Path to the file to edit (relative or absolute)

oldText.description: Exact text for one targeted replacement. It must be unique in the original file and must not overlap with any other edits[].oldText in the same call.

newText.description: Replacement text for this targeted edit.

edits.description: One or more targeted replacements. Each edit is matched against the original file, not incrementally. Do not include overlapping or nested edits. If two changes touch the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead.

tools.name: write
tools.description: Write content to a file. Creates the file if it doesn't exist, overwrites if it does. Automatically creates parent directories.

path.description: Path to the file to write (relative or absolute)

content.description: Content to write to the file

tools.name: read
tools.description: Read the contents of a file. Supports text files and images (jpg, png, gif, webp). Images are sent as attachments. For text files, output is truncated to 2000 lines or 50KB (whichever is hit first). Use offset/limit for large files. When you need the full file, continue with offset until complete.

path.description: Path to the file to read (relative or absolute)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read

tools.name: bash
tools.description: Execute a bash command in the current working directory. Returns stdout and stderr. Output is truncated to last 2000 lines or 50KB (whichever is hit first). If truncated, full output is saved to a temp file. Optionally provide a timeout in seconds.

timeout.description: Timeout in seconds (optional, no default timeout)

tools.name: edit
tools.description: Edit a single file using exact text replacement. Every edits[].oldText must match a unique, non-overlapping region of the original file. If two changes affect the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead of emitting overlapping edits. Do not include large unchanged regions just to connect distant changes.

path.description: Path to the file to edit (relative or absolute)

oldText.description: Exact text for one targeted replacement. It must be unique in the original file and must not overlap with any other edits[].oldText in the same call.

newText.description: Replacement text for this targeted edit.

edits.description: One or more targeted replacements. Each edit is matched against the original file, not incrementally. Do not include overlapping or nested edits. If two changes touch the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead.

tools.name: write
tools.description: Write content to a file. Creates the file if it doesn't exist, overwrites if it does. Automatically creates parent directories.

path.description: Path to the file to write (relative or absolute)

content.description: Content to write to the file

input.content:
You are an expert coding assistant operating inside pi, a coding agent harness. You help users by reading files, executing commands, editing code, and writing new files.

Available tools:
- read: Read file contents
- bash: Execute bash commands (ls, grep, find, etc.)
- edit: Make precise file edits with exact text replacement, including multiple disjoint edits in one call
- write: Create or overwrite files

In addition to the tools above, you may have access to other custom tools depending on the project.

Guidelines:
- Use bash for file operations like ls, rg, find
- Use read to examine files instead of cat or sed.
- Use edit for precise changes (edits[].oldText must match exactly)
- When changing multiple separate locations in one file, use one edit call with multiple entries in edits[] instead of multiple edit calls
- Each edits[].oldText is matched against the original file, not after earlier edits are applied. Do not emit overlapping or nested edits. Merge nearby changes into one edit.
- Keep edits[].oldText as small as possible while still being unique in the file. Do not pad with large unchanged regions.
- Use write only for new files or complete rewrites.
- Be concise in your responses
- Show file paths clearly when working with files

Pi documentation (read only when the user asks about pi itself, its SDK, extensions, themes, skills, or TUI):
- Main documentation: /Work/langsmith-pi-extension/node_modules/.pnpm/@earendil-works+pi-coding-agent@0.78.0_ws@8.20.0_zod@4.4.3/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/README.md
- Additional docs: /Work/langsmith-pi-extension/node_modules/.pnpm/@earendil-works+pi-coding-agent@0.78.0_ws@8.20.0_zod@4.4.3/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/docs
- Examples: /Work/langsmith-pi-extension/node_modules/.pnpm/@earendil-works+pi-coding-agent@0.78.0_ws@8.20.0_zod@4.4.3/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/examples (extensions, custom tools, SDK)
- When reading pi docs or examples, resolve docs/... under Additional docs and examples/... under Examples, not the current working directory
- When asked about: extensions (docs/extensions.md, examples/extensions/), themes (docs/themes.md), skills (docs/skills.md), prompt templates (docs/prompt-templates.md), TUI components (docs/tui.md), keybindings (docs/keybindings.md), SDK integrations (docs/sdk.md), custom providers (docs/custom-provider.md), adding models (docs/models.md), pi packages (docs/packages.md)
- When working on pi topics, read the docs and examples, and follow .md cross-references before implementing
- Always read pi .md files completely and follow links to related docs (e.g., tui.md for TUI API details)

The following skills provide specialized instructions for specific tasks.
Use the read tool to load a skill's file when the task matches its description.
When a skill file references a relative path, resolve it against the skill directory (parent of SKILL.md / dirname of the path) and use that absolute path in tool commands.

- read: Read file contents
- bash: Execute bash commands (ls, grep, find, etc.)
- edit: Make precise file edits with exact text replacement, including multiple disjoint edits in one call
- write: Create or overwrite files

Guidelines:
- Use bash for file operations like ls, rg, find
- Use read to examine files instead of cat or sed.
- Use edit for precise changes (edits[].oldText must match exactly)
- When changing multiple separate locations in one file, use one edit call with multiple entries in edits[] instead of multiple edit calls
- Each edits[].oldText is matched against the original file, not after earlier edits are applied. Do not emit overlapping or nested edits. Merge nearby changes into one edit.
- Keep edits[].oldText as small as possible while still being unique in the file. Do not pad with large unchanged regions.
- Use write only for new files or complete rewrites.
- Be concise in your responses
- Show file paths clearly when working with files

input.content:
You are an expert coding assistant operating inside pi, a coding agent harness. You help users by reading files, executing commands, editing code, and writing new files.

Available tools:
- read: Read file contents
- bash: Execute bash commands (ls, grep, find, etc.)
- edit: Make precise file edits with exact text replacement, including multiple disjoint edits in one call
- write: Create or overwrite files

In addition to the tools above, you may have access to other custom tools depending on the project.

Guidelines:
- Use bash for file operations like ls, rg, find
- Use read to examine files instead of cat or sed.
- Use edit for precise changes (edits[].oldText must match exactly)
- When changing multiple separate locations in one file, use one edit call with multiple entries in edits[] instead of multiple edit calls
- Each edits[].oldText is matched against the original file, not after earlier edits are applied. Do not emit overlapping or nested edits. Merge nearby changes into one edit.
- Keep edits[].oldText as small as possible while still being unique in the file. Do not pad with large unchanged regions.
- Use write only for new files or complete rewrites.
- Be concise in your responses
- Show file paths clearly when working with files

tools.name: read
tools.description: Read the contents of a file. Supports text files and images (jpg, png, gif, webp). Images are sent as attachments. For text files, output is truncated to 2000 lines or 50KB (whichever is hit first). Use offset/limit for large files. When you need the full file, continue with offset until complete.

path.description: Path to the file to read (relative or absolute)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read

tools.name: bash
tools.description: Execute a bash command in the current working directory. Returns stdout and stderr. Output is truncated to last 2000 lines or 50KB (whichever is hit first). If truncated, full output is saved to a temp file. Optionally provide a timeout in seconds.

timeout.description: Timeout in seconds (optional, no default timeout)

tools.name: edit
tools.description: Edit a single file using exact text replacement. Every edits[].oldText must match a unique, non-overlapping region of the original file. If two changes affect the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead of emitting overlapping edits. Do not include large unchanged regions just to connect distant changes.

path.description: Path to the file to edit (relative or absolute)

oldText.description: Exact text for one targeted replacement. It must be unique in the original file and must not overlap with any other edits[].oldText in the same call.

newText.description: Replacement text for this targeted edit.

edits.description: One or more targeted replacements. Each edit is matched against the original file, not incrementally. Do not include overlapping or nested edits. If two changes touch the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead.

tools.name: write
tools.description: Write content to a file. Creates the file if it doesn't exist, overwrites if it does. Automatically creates parent directories.

path.description: Path to the file to write (relative or absolute)

content.description: Content to write to the file

You are an expert coding assistant operating inside pi, a coding agent harness. You help users by reading files, executing commands, editing code, and writing new files.

Available tools:
- read: Read file contents
- bash: Execute bash commands (ls, grep, find, etc.)
- edit: Make precise file edits with exact text replacement, including multiple disjoint edits in one call
- write: Create or overwrite files

In addition to the tools above, you may have access to other custom tools depending on the project.

Guidelines:
- Use bash for file operations like ls, rg, find
- Use read to examine files instead of cat or sed.
- Use edit for precise changes (edits[].oldText must match exactly)
- When changing multiple separate locations in one file, use one edit call with multiple entries in edits[] instead of multiple edit calls
- Each edits[].oldText is matched against the original file, not after earlier edits are applied. Do not emit overlapping or nested edits. Merge nearby changes into one edit.
- Keep edits[].oldText as small as possible while still being unique in the file. Do not pad with large unchanged regions.
- Use write only for new files or complete rewrites.
- Be concise in your responses
- Show file paths clearly when working with files

Pi documentation (read only when the user asks about pi itself, its SDK, extensions, themes, skills, or TUI):
- Main documentation: /Work/langsmith-pi-extension/node_modules/.pnpm/@earendil-works+pi-coding-agent@0.78.0_ws@8.20.0_zod@4.4.3/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/README.md
- Additional docs: /Work/langsmith-pi-extension/node_modules/.pnpm/@earendil-works+pi-coding-agent@0.78.0_ws@8.20.0_zod@4.4.3/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/docs
- Examples: /Work/langsmith-pi-extension/node_modules/.pnpm/@earendil-works+pi-coding-agent@0.78.0_ws@8.20.0_zod@4.4.3/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/examples (extensions, custom tools, SDK)
- When reading pi docs or examples, resolve docs/... under Additional docs and examples/... under Examples, not the current working directory
- When asked about: extensions (docs/extensions.md, examples/extensions/), themes (docs/themes.md), skills (docs/skills.md), prompt templates (docs/prompt-templates.md), TUI components (docs/tui.md), keybindings (docs/keybindings.md), SDK integrations (docs/sdk.md), custom providers (docs/custom-provider.md), adding models (docs/models.md), pi packages (docs/packages.md)
- When working on pi topics, read the docs and examples, and follow .md cross-references before implementing
- Always read pi .md files completely and follow links to related docs (e.g., tui.md for TUI API details)

The following skills provide specialized instructions for specific tasks.
Use the read tool to load a skill's file when the task matches its description.
When a skill file references a relative path, resolve it against the skill directory (parent of SKILL.md / dirname of the path) and use that absolute path in tool commands.

tools.name: read
tools.description: Read the contents of a file. Supports text files and images (jpg, png, gif, webp). Images are sent as attachments. For text files, output is truncated to 2000 lines or 50KB (whichever is hit first). Use offset/limit for large files. When you need the full file, continue with offset until complete.

path.description: Path to the file to read (relative or absolute)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read

tools.name: bash
tools.description: Execute a bash command in the current working directory. Returns stdout and stderr. Output is truncated to last 2000 lines or 50KB (whichever is hit first). If truncated, full output is saved to a temp file. Optionally provide a timeout in seconds.

timeout.description: Timeout in seconds (optional, no default timeout)

tools.name: edit
tools.description: Edit a single file using exact text replacement. Every edits[].oldText must match a unique, non-overlapping region of the original file. If two changes affect the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead of emitting overlapping edits. Do not include large unchanged regions just to connect distant changes.

path.description: Path to the file to edit (relative or absolute)

oldText.description: Exact text for one targeted replacement. It must be unique in the original file and must not overlap with any other edits[].oldText in the same call.

newText.description: Replacement text for this targeted edit.

edits.description: One or more targeted replacements. Each edit is matched against the original file, not incrementally. Do not include overlapping or nested edits. If two changes touch the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead.

tools.name: write
tools.description: Write content to a file. Creates the file if it doesn't exist, overwrites if it does. Automatically creates parent directories.

path.description: Path to the file to write (relative or absolute)

content.description: Content to write to the file

content.text:
This is a small TypeScript package named `@langchain/langsmith-pi-extension`. It is a **LangSmith tracing extension for the Pi Coding Agent**.

The README is currently just:

```md
# LangSmith Pi Extension

WIP
```

So most of the project’s meaning comes from the source code.

## High-level purpose

The project integrates **Pi Coding Agent** with **LangSmith** by listening to Pi extension lifecycle events and turning them into LangSmith traces.

When enabled, it records a Pi agent session as a LangSmith run tree:

```text
Pi agent run / Pi subagent run
  ├─ Pi turn 0
  │   ├─ LLM provider request
  │   ├─ tool call
  │   └─ ...
  ├─ Pi turn 1
  │   └─ ...
  └─ final outputs / errors / shutdown metadata
```

It captures:

- user prompt and image count
- working directory metadata
- selected provider/model
- LLM request payloads
- assistant outputs
- token/cost usage metadata
- tool execution inputs/outputs/errors
- turn boundaries
- agent completion/shutdown
- subagent trace relationships

## Main files

### `src/index.ts`

This is the main Pi extension entrypoint.

It exports a default async function that receives the Pi `ExtensionAPI` and registers event handlers.

Key behavior:

- Reads config via `getConfig()`
- Creates a LangSmith `Client`
- Registers a Pi command:

  ```ts
  langsmith-tracing
  ```

  which reports whether tracing is enabled.

- Sets Pi UI status such as:

  ```text
  LangSmith: tracing
  LangSmith: disabled
  LangSmith: tracing run
  LangSmith: traced
  ```

- On `before_agent_start`, creates the root LangSmith `RunTree`
- On `turn_start`, creates a child chain run for each Pi turn
- On `before_provider_request`, creates or queues an LLM run
- On `message_end`, ends the LLM run and attaches usage metadata
- On `tool_execution_start`, creates a tool run
- On `tool_execution_update`, appends tool update events
- On `tool_execution_end`, ends the tool run
- On `agent_end`, closes dangling runs and finalizes the root run
- On `session_shutdown`, handles incomplete traces and flushes LangSmith batches

It also has special support for Pi subagents. If the `subagent` tool is called, it writes parent trace context into environment variables so child/subagent processes can attach their LangSmith traces under the parent tool run.

Relevant env vars used internally:

```ts
LANGSMITH_PI_PARENT_DOTTED_ORDER
LANGSMITH_PI_PARENT_BAGGAGE
```

Subagent metadata is read from:

```ts
PI_SUBAGENT_CHILD
PI_SUBAGENT_RUN_ID
PI_SUBAGENT_CHILD_AGENT
PI_SUBAGENT_CHILD_INDEX
PI_SUBAGENT_DEPTH
```

### `src/config.ts`

This handles configuration.

It supports config from:

1. Defaults
2. Global config file:

   ```text
   ~/.pi/langsmith.json
   ```

3. Local project config file:

   ```text
   .pi/langsmith.json
   ```

4. Environment variables, which take precedence

The final config schema includes:

```ts
{
  enabled: boolean;
  api_key?: string;
  api_url?: string;
  project?: string;
  metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
  replicas?: Array<{
    api_url?: string;
    api_key?: string;
    project?: string;
    updates?: Record<string, unknown>;
  }>;
}
```

Supported environment variables include:

```text
TRACE_TO_LANGSMITH
LANGSMITH_PI_API_KEY
LANGSMITH_API_KEY
LANGSMITH_PI_ENDPOINT
LANGSMITH_ENDPOINT
LANGSMITH_PI_PROJECT
LANGSMITH_PROJECT
LANGSMITH_PI_METADATA
LANGSMITH_METADATA
LANGSMITH_PI_RUNS_ENDPOINTS
LANGSMITH_RUNS_ENDPOINTS
```

Default behavior is:

```ts
enabled: false
project: "pi-coding-agent"
```

So tracing is opt-in.

### `src/record.ts`

This is a helper/debug extension that records Pi events to a local JSONL file:

```text
langsmith.jsonl
```

It listens to many Pi events and appends entries like:

```ts
[
  Date.now(),
  eventName,
  eventArg,
  {
    cwd,
    model,
    getContextUsage
  }
]
```

This appears to be used for replay/testing the extension behavior.

### `test/replay.test.ts`

This test reads `langsmith.jsonl`, registers the main extension, and replays the captured Pi events through the extension handlers.

It uses:

- `vitest`
- `langsmith`
- Pi extension types

The test is called:

```ts
it("replay", { timeout: 60_000 }, async () => { ... })
```

It constructs a mock `ExtensionAPI`, registers handlers, then invokes them from the JSONL event log.

## Package setup

From `package.json`:

```json
{
  "name": "@langchain/langsmith-pi-extension",
  "version": "0.0.1",
  "description": "LangSmith extension for Pi Coding Agent",
  "type": "module",
  "license": "MIT",
  "author": "LangChain"
}
```

Runtime dependencies:

- `langsmith`
- `zod`

Dev dependencies:

- `@earendil-works/pi-ai`
- `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent`
- `vitest`
- `oxfmt`
- `@types/node`

The package declares itself as a Pi package/extension via:

```json
"pi": {
  "extensions": [
    "./src/index.ts"
  ]
}
```

There are currently no npm/pnpm scripts defined.

## Current project state

This looks like an early/WIP implementation of a LangSmith integration for Pi. The main tracing logic is already substantial, but the surrounding package is minimal:

- README is unfinished
- no build/test scripts in `package.json`
- no visible TypeScript config
- `langsmith.jsonl` is checked into the working tree as a replay fixture/event log
- `src/index.ts` still contains some debug `console.log` calls

Also, there is a `.env` file present in the repo root; I did not inspect it. Given this project involves API keys, it should be treated carefully and probably kept out of version control.

- edit: Make precise file edits with exact text replacement, including multiple disjoint edits in one call
- write: Create or overwrite files

In addition to the tools above, you may have access to other custom tools depending on the project.

Guidelines:
- Use bash for file operations like ls, rg, find
- Use read to examine files instead of cat or sed.
- Use edit for precise changes (edits[].oldText must match exactly)
- When changing multiple separate locations in one file, use one edit call with multiple entries in edits[] instead of multiple edit calls

- Show file paths clearly when working with files

- Main documentation: /Work/langsmith-pi-extension/node_modules/.pnpm/@earendil-works+pi-coding-agent@0.78.0_ws@8.20.0_zod@4.4.3/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/README.md

- When asked about: extensions (docs/extensions.md, examples/extensions/), themes (docs/themes.md), skills (docs/skills.md), prompt templates (docs/prompt-templates.md), TUI components (docs/tui.md), keybindings (docs/keybindings.md), SDK integrations (docs/sdk.md), custom providers (docs/custom-provider.md), adding models (docs/models.md), pi packages (docs/packages.md)
- When working on pi topics, read the docs and examples, and follow .md cross-references before implementing

This is a small TypeScript package named `@langchain/langsmith-pi-extension`. It is a **LangSmith tracing extension for the Pi Coding Agent**.

The README is currently just:

```md
# LangSmith Pi Extension

WIP
```

So most of the project’s meaning comes from the source code.

## High-level purpose

The project integrates **Pi Coding Agent** with **LangSmith** by listening to Pi extension lifecycle events and turning them into LangSmith traces.

When enabled, it records a Pi agent session as a LangSmith run tree:

```text
Pi agent run / Pi subagent run
  ├─ Pi turn 0
  │   ├─ LLM provider request
  │   ├─ tool call
  │   └─ ...
  ├─ Pi turn 1
  │   └─ ...
  └─ final outputs / errors / shutdown metadata
```

It captures:

- user prompt and image count
- working directory metadata
- selected provider/model
- LLM request payloads
- assistant outputs
- token/cost usage metadata
- tool execution inputs/outputs/errors
- turn boundaries
- agent completion/shutdown
- subagent trace relationships

## Main files

### `src/index.ts`

This is the main Pi extension entrypoint.

It exports a default async function that receives the Pi `ExtensionAPI` and registers event handlers.

Key behavior:

- Reads config via `getConfig()`
- Creates a LangSmith `Client`
- Registers a Pi command:

  ```ts
  langsmith-tracing
  ```

  which reports whether tracing is enabled.

- Sets Pi UI status such as:

  ```text
  LangSmith: tracing
  LangSmith: disabled
  LangSmith: tracing run
  LangSmith: traced
  ```

- On `before_agent_start`, creates the root LangSmith `RunTree`
- On `turn_start`, creates a child chain run for each Pi turn
- On `before_provider_request`, creates or queues an LLM run
- On `message_end`, ends the LLM run and attaches usage metadata
- On `tool_execution_start`, creates a tool run
- On `tool_execution_update`, appends tool update events
- On `tool_execution_end`, ends the tool run
- On `agent_end`, closes dangling runs and finalizes the root run
- On `session_shutdown`, handles incomplete traces and flushes LangSmith batches

It also has special support for Pi subagents. If the `subagent` tool is called, it writes parent trace context into environment variables so child/subagent processes can attach their LangSmith traces under the parent tool run.

Relevant env vars used internally:

```ts
LANGSMITH_PI_PARENT_DOTTED_ORDER
LANGSMITH_PI_PARENT_BAGGAGE
```

Subagent metadata is read from:

```ts
PI_SUBAGENT_CHILD
PI_SUBAGENT_RUN_ID
PI_SUBAGENT_CHILD_AGENT
PI_SUBAGENT_CHILD_INDEX
PI_SUBAGENT_DEPTH
```

### `src/config.ts`

This handles configuration.

It supports config from:

1. Defaults
2. Global config file:

   ```text
   ~/.pi/langsmith.json
   ```

3. Local project config file:

   ```text
   .pi/langsmith.json
   ```

4. Environment variables, which take precedence

The final config schema includes:

```ts
{
  enabled: boolean;
  api_key?: string;
  api_url?: string;
  project?: string;
  metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
  replicas?: Array<{
    api_url?: string;
    api_key?: string;
    project?: string;
    updates?: Record<string, unknown>;
  }>;
}
```

Supported environment variables include:

```text
TRACE_TO_LANGSMITH
LANGSMITH_PI_API_KEY
LANGSMITH_API_KEY
LANGSMITH_PI_ENDPOINT
LANGSMITH_ENDPOINT
LANGSMITH_PI_PROJECT
LANGSMITH_PROJECT
LANGSMITH_PI_METADATA
LANGSMITH_METADATA
LANGSMITH_PI_RUNS_ENDPOINTS
LANGSMITH_RUNS_ENDPOINTS
```

Default behavior is:

```ts
enabled: false
project: "pi-coding-agent"
```

So tracing is opt-in.

### `src/record.ts`

This is a helper/debug extension that records Pi events to a local JSONL file:

```text
langsmith.jsonl
```

It listens to many Pi events and appends entries like:

```ts
[
  Date.now(),
  eventName,
  eventArg,
  {
    cwd,
    model,
    getContextUsage
  }
]
```

This appears to be used for replay/testing the extension behavior.

### `test/replay.test.ts`

This test reads `langsmith.jsonl`, registers the main extension, and replays the captured Pi events through the extension handlers.

It uses:

- `vitest`
- `langsmith`
- Pi extension types

The test is called:

```ts
it("replay", { timeout: 60_000 }, async () => { ... })
```

It constructs a mock `ExtensionAPI`, registers handlers, then invokes them from the JSONL event log.

## Package setup

From `package.json`:

```json
{
  "name": "@langchain/langsmith-pi-extension",
  "version": "0.0.1",
  "description": "LangSmith extension for Pi Coding Agent",
  "type": "module",
  "license": "MIT",
  "author": "LangChain"
}
```

Runtime dependencies:

- `langsmith`
- `zod`

Dev dependencies:

- `@earendil-works/pi-ai`
- `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent`
- `vitest`
- `oxfmt`
- `@types/node`

The package declares itself as a Pi package/extension via:

```json
"pi": {
  "extensions": [
    "./src/index.ts"
  ]
}
```

There are currently no npm/pnpm scripts defined.

## Current project state

This looks like an early/WIP implementation of a LangSmith integration for Pi. The main tracing logic is already substantial, but the surrounding package is minimal:

- README is unfinished
- no build/test scripts in `package.json`
- no visible TypeScript config
- `langsmith.jsonl` is checked into the working tree as a replay fixture/event log
- `src/index.ts` still contains some debug `console.log` calls

Also, there is a `.env` file present in the repo root; I did not inspect it. Given this project involves API keys, it should be treated carefully and probably kept out of version control.
